I have been working on a comprehensive lesson plan for an advanced English writing class and I would appreciate it if you could review the complete structure, including the warm-up, the main instructional sequence, the collaborative activity, the individual writing task, and the closing reflection prompt, and let me know if the progression feels logical, if the instructions are appropriately worded for upper-intermediate learners, and whether the time allocations I have set for each section seem realistic given a ninety-minute class period.

Could you please evaluate the full set of grading criteria I developed for a project-based learning assignment in which students are asked to research a social issue, create a multimedia presentation, and deliver a five-minute oral summary, and tell me whether the criteria cover all relevant dimensions of performance, whether the descriptors for each level are sufficiently distinct to allow for consistent scoring, and whether there are any aspects of the task that I have overlooked or underweighted in the rubric?

I have drafted a detailed unit overview for a six-week module on environmental science that includes the core concepts to be covered each week, the key vocabulary terms students should acquire, the formative assessments I plan to use, and the summative task at the end of the unit, and I would like you to review all of this and tell me whether the scope and sequence is coherent, whether the assessments are appropriately aligned with the stated learning outcomes, and whether the vocabulary load seems manageable for students at this level.

Please review the series of comprehension questions I wrote to accompany a challenging academic text on global economic inequality, considering whether the questions progress appropriately from literal recall to inferential and evaluative thinking, whether the wording of each question is clear and unambiguous, whether any questions are too leading or make assumptions that could bias student responses, and whether the overall set provides a fair and comprehensive measure of student understanding of the source material.

I am preparing a professional development workshop for teachers on the topic of formative assessment and I have written a full session outline including an icebreaker, a reflective activity, a collaborative task, and a presentation segment, and I would like you to read through everything and give me feedback on whether the session flows well, whether the activities are likely to generate meaningful professional discussion, whether the materials I plan to distribute are clear and practical, and whether the session appropriately balances theory with hands-on practice.

Could you review the self-assessment checklist I designed for secondary school students to use after completing a research project, and let me know whether the language is accessible for teenagers, whether the checklist covers all the key skills involved in the research process, whether the items are specific enough to be actionable rather than vague, whether there are any important criteria I have left out, and whether the format of the checklist encourages genuine reflection rather than superficial self-rating?

I have written a detailed explanation of the scientific method intended for Year 8 students who are encountering formal research concepts for the first time, and I would appreciate your feedback on whether the explanation uses age-appropriate language throughout, whether the examples I chose to illustrate each stage of the process are relatable and easy to visualise, whether the structure of the explanation is logical and easy to follow, and whether there are any misconceptions embedded in my wording that could confuse students rather than helping them understand.

Please assess the full script I wrote for a fifteen-minute instructional video on persuasive writing techniques, taking into account whether the tone is appropriate for a general secondary school audience, whether the key concepts are introduced in a sensible order, whether the examples of persuasive language featured in the script are authentic and varied enough to be genuinely illustrative, whether the transitions between sections feel natural, and whether the closing summary effectively reinforces the main learning points from the video.

I have created a set of ten scenario-based discussion prompts for a training course on workplace communication and I would like you to review all of them together to check whether the scenarios represent a realistic and diverse range of professional situations, whether the prompts are open-ended enough to encourage extended discussion rather than single-answer responses, whether any of the scenarios contain cultural assumptions that could make them feel exclusionary, and whether the set as a whole covers the range of communication challenges the course is intended to address.

Could you evaluate the student feedback report template I developed for use by tutors at the end of each term, which includes sections for commenting on academic progress, participation and engagement, areas for development, and goals for the next term, and tell me whether the template gives tutors enough guidance to write substantive and constructive feedback, whether the categories are clearly defined and mutually exclusive, whether the template strikes the right balance between structure and flexibility, and whether there is anything important about student development that the template fails to capture?

I have designed a differentiated reading activity for a mixed-ability class where students at three different levels work with different versions of the same news article, and I would appreciate your feedback on whether the three versions are appropriately matched to the target proficiency levels, whether the comprehension tasks associated with each version are suitably challenging for their respective groups, whether the activity could be managed practically within a standard lesson, and whether there are any equity concerns about the way I have structured the differentiation that I should address.

Please review the parent information letter I wrote explaining the new project-based learning approach being introduced in the school this semester, and tell me whether the letter is written in accessible language that avoids jargon, whether it gives parents a clear picture of what to expect and how they can support their children, whether the tone is welcoming and inclusive without being condescending, whether all the key logistical information parents would need is included, and whether there is anything about the letter that might cause unnecessary confusion or anxiety.

I have put together a comprehensive exam preparation guide for students sitting a high-stakes English language proficiency test and I would like you to go through it carefully and advise me on whether the revision strategies I have included are evidence-based and practical, whether the practice exercises are representative of the actual exam format, whether the guide is well-organised and easy for students to navigate independently, whether the language used to explain technical test-taking concepts is clear enough for non-native speakers, and whether there are any important exam skills or strategies that I have neglected to include.

Could you review the problem-based learning scenario I designed for a university-level business ethics course in which student teams must advise a fictional company facing a series of ethical dilemmas across multiple case memos, and tell me whether the dilemmas presented are sufficiently realistic and complex to generate meaningful ethical debate, whether the case materials provide enough context for students to engage deeply without requiring specialist knowledge, whether the task instructions are clear and appropriately scoped for a semester-long group project, and whether the assessment criteria I have specified capture the full range of skills the task is intended to develop?

I have written a teacher reflection journal template designed to help educators systematically document and evaluate their own instructional practices over the course of a school year and I would like you to review it and share your thoughts on whether the reflection prompts are likely to surface genuine professional insight rather than surface-level commentary, whether the structure of the journal supports ongoing professional growth rather than just documentation, whether the language of the prompts is appropriately professional without being bureaucratic, and whether there are key dimensions of teaching practice that the template currently fails to address.

Please review the gamified vocabulary review activity I created for an intermediate Spanish language class where students earn points by correctly using target vocabulary in original sentences during a team-based game, and tell me whether the rules of the game are explained clearly enough for students to understand without a lengthy verbal explanation, whether the activity is likely to generate genuine language production rather than rote recall, whether the scoring system is fair and motivating, whether the vocabulary items selected for the game are appropriately challenging for the target level, and whether there are any logistical issues with running the activity in a class of twenty-five students.

I have drafted a full set of observation notes and evaluation comments following a lesson observation I conducted of a trainee teacher, covering classroom management, instructional delivery, student engagement, use of questioning, and responsiveness to student needs, and I would like you to review the notes and tell me whether my feedback is specific and evidence-based rather than vague and impressionistic, whether I have struck an appropriate balance between identifying strengths and areas for development, whether the language I have used is professional and constructive, and whether the overall picture I have painted is likely to be genuinely helpful to the trainee in improving their practice.

Could you evaluate the blended learning module I developed for an online professional development course for healthcare trainers, which includes a series of pre-reading articles, video lectures, reflective discussion tasks, a group case study analysis, and a final individual assessment, and tell me whether the module design reflects sound principles of adult learning, whether the workload across the various components is reasonably balanced, whether the assessment is well-aligned with the stated competencies, and whether there are any technical or pedagogical risks in the way I have structured the online learning experience that I should address before the course launches?

I have written a detailed set of instructions for a collaborative research project in which student groups must investigate a local community issue, interview stakeholders, analyse their findings, and produce a written report and oral presentation, and I would like you to review these instructions and advise me on whether students will have a clear understanding of what is expected at each stage, whether the criteria for success are sufficiently transparent, whether the timeline is realistic given the other demands on students, whether the instructions adequately address how group dynamics and individual accountability will be managed, and whether there are any potential ethical issues with the community engagement component that I should flag to students in advance.

Please review the curriculum map I created for a one-year interdisciplinary humanities course covering history, literature, and cultural studies, which outlines the thematic units, key texts, central questions, and assessment tasks for each term, and tell me whether the thematic connections across the three disciplines feel genuine and intellectually coherent rather than forced, whether the balance between the three subject areas seems appropriate, whether the assessment tasks are diverse and varied enough across the year, whether the difficulty curve across the three terms is well-calibrated, and whether there are any significant gaps in representation or perspective in the texts and topics I have selected.

I need your feedback on the detailed project specification I wrote for a capstone assignment in a digital marketing course where students must develop a full marketing strategy for a real local business, including market analysis, target audience profiling, channel strategy, content plan, and evaluation framework, and I would like to know whether the scope of the project is manageable within the time available, whether the instructions for each component are clear and specific enough, whether the assessment criteria adequately reflect the complexity of the task, and whether there are any aspects of professional marketing practice that the project fails to incorporate.

Could you review the annotated bibliography and discussion guide I prepared to support a university seminar on post-colonial literature, which includes summaries of key scholarly texts, a set of close reading activities, and a series of seminar discussion questions designed to provoke critical thinking about power, identity, and representation, and tell me whether the selection of scholarly sources is appropriately diverse and current, whether the close reading activities are likely to generate the kind of analytical thinking I am aiming for, whether the discussion questions are provocative without being leading, and whether the overall package will give students sufficient intellectual preparation for an advanced seminar discussion?

I have created a comprehensive induction programme for new teaching staff joining a secondary school, which spans the first term and includes orientation sessions, classroom observation opportunities, mentoring meetings, professional reading, and reflective tasks, and I would appreciate your feedback on whether the programme is likely to make new teachers feel welcomed and supported, whether the workload during induction is reasonable given that new teachers are simultaneously preparing and delivering lessons, whether the mentoring structure is clearly defined, and whether the programme adequately addresses the professional knowledge and practical skills that new secondary school teachers most urgently need.

Please assess the student learning profile template I developed to help teachers at the start of a new year gather information about each learner's strengths, challenges, preferred ways of working, and personal interests that could be leveraged in lesson planning, and tell me whether the questions are likely to elicit genuinely useful pedagogical information, whether the format is accessible and manageable for students of varying literacy levels, whether any of the questions might feel intrusive or uncomfortable for students to answer, and whether the template could realistically be used by a busy teacher to inform differentiated planning rather than just filed and forgotten.

I have written a detailed course syllabus for a graduate-level course on research methods in education, including the course overview, learning outcomes, weekly schedule, assessment breakdown, academic integrity policy, and guidance on participation expectations, and I would like you to review the entire document and tell me whether the learning outcomes are appropriately ambitious for graduate study, whether the assessment tasks are well-designed to develop and measure research competence, whether the weekly schedule is realistic and coherent, whether the tone of the syllabus is appropriately professional and welcoming, and whether there is any important information that graduate students would typically expect in a course syllabus that I have failed to include.

Could you evaluate the set of role-play scenarios I designed for a teacher training programme on managing difficult conversations with parents, where trainees must practise responding to scenarios involving parental complaints, concerns about assessment, and disagreements about student behaviour management, and let me know whether the scenarios are realistic and nuanced enough to prepare trainees for actual situations they might encounter, whether they cover an appropriately diverse range of challenges, whether the briefing notes give trainees enough context to engage meaningfully, and whether the debriefing questions I have provided are likely to generate useful professional reflection after the role-play activities?

I have drafted a series of student-facing anchor charts summarising the key writing conventions for five different text types covered in an upper primary English curriculum, and I would like your feedback on whether the information on each chart is accurate and comprehensive without being overwhelming, whether the language used is pitched appropriately for ten to twelve year olds, whether the visual organisation of each chart makes it easy to scan and use as a quick reference during writing tasks, and whether the examples I have included on each chart effectively illustrate the conventions being described.

Please review the complete assessment package I developed for a listening skills unit in an advanced ESL course, which includes a set of listening tasks using authentic audio sources, a range of question types targeting different listening subskills, a marking scheme, and a set of teacher notes on administering the assessment, and tell me whether the audio materials are appropriate in terms of accent variety, speed, and topic relevance, whether the task types are well-matched to the subskills they are intended to assess, whether the marking scheme is sufficiently clear and detailed to support reliable scoring, and whether the teacher notes cover everything that an examiner would need to know to administer the assessment consistently.

I have created a professional learning community meeting agenda and facilitation guide for a team of primary school teachers who will be collaborating to analyse student writing samples and plan differentiated instruction, and I would appreciate your feedback on whether the agenda allocates time appropriately across the different stages of the collaborative inquiry process, whether the facilitation prompts are likely to keep the discussion productive and evidence-focused, whether the tools and protocols I have included are well-suited to a group of primary teachers with varying levels of experience in data-driven teaching, and whether the overall design of the meeting is likely to result in actionable instructional decisions.

Could you review the media literacy curriculum I developed for a Year 9 class that covers source evaluation, identifying bias, understanding how algorithms shape information exposure, and creating responsible digital content, and tell me whether the sequence of topics is pedagogically sound and builds on prior knowledge in a logical way, whether the activities I have planned are likely to be relevant and engaging for contemporary teenagers, whether the curriculum adequately addresses the ethical dimensions of media consumption and production, whether there are any significant contemporary media literacy issues that the curriculum overlooks, and whether the assessment tasks are well-designed to measure the specific skills and understandings the curriculum aims to develop?

I have written a complete teachers guide to accompany a published student workbook on mathematical problem solving, which includes lesson notes, worked examples, common misconceptions to watch out for, extension activities, and suggested homework tasks for each chapter, and I would like you to review the guide and advise me on whether the lesson notes give teachers enough pedagogical guidance without being overly prescriptive, whether the worked examples model effective mathematical reasoning clearly, whether the misconceptions identified are the ones that genuinely arise most frequently with students at this level, and whether the extension and homework tasks are appropriately challenging and varied.

Please evaluate the full course outline I have developed for a four-week intensive academic writing course for international postgraduate students, including the session topics, key skills to be developed, reading materials, writing tasks, and formative feedback mechanisms, and tell me whether the pace of skill development across the four weeks is realistic for students who may not have written in an academic English context before, whether the writing tasks progress in a logical and scaffolded way, whether there is sufficient practice time built in for the skills being taught, and whether the reading materials I have selected are genuinely useful models of the academic writing conventions students need to acquire.

I have designed a student-led parent showcase event where learners present their portfolio work from the semester to their families, and I have written the complete event programme including student presentation guidelines, portfolio curation instructions, reflection prompts, and a guide for families on how to engage meaningfully with their child's work, and I would like your feedback on whether the event structure is likely to create a genuinely celebratory and educational experience, whether the instructions for students are clear and appropriately challenging, whether the family engagement guide is accessible to parents with varying levels of educational background, and whether there are any logistical or inclusivity considerations I should address.

Could you review the teacher evaluation observation form I have developed for use by school administrators conducting formal performance reviews of teaching staff, which covers lesson planning, instructional delivery, classroom environment, assessment practices, and professional responsibilities, and tell me whether the criteria are clearly defined and observable rather than vague and subjective, whether the rating scale I have used is appropriately calibrated to distinguish meaningfully between performance levels, whether the form is practical to complete during a live lesson observation, whether there are any aspects of effective teaching that the form currently fails to capture, and whether the language and framing of the criteria are likely to be perceived as fair and professionally respectful by teachers being evaluated?

I have written a detailed explanation of the concept of cognitive load for trainee teachers attending a workshop on inclusive instructional design, and I would like you to review it and tell me whether the explanation of the underlying theory is accurate and appropriately simplified for an audience without a psychology background, whether the practical classroom examples I have used to illustrate the concept are relatable and helpful, whether the explanation clearly connects the theory to actionable teaching strategies, whether the language is pitched at the right level for trainee teachers who are new to educational research, and whether there are any important nuances of the cognitive load framework that I have omitted or misrepresented.

Please assess the series of formative assessment strategies I compiled as a practical guide for subject teachers in a large secondary school, which includes exit tickets, mini whiteboards, traffic light cards, think-pair-share, and concept mapping, and tell me whether the guide explains each strategy clearly enough for a teacher who has never used it before, whether the examples I have provided for each strategy are specific and practically useful, whether the guide addresses common implementation challenges and how to overcome them, whether the strategies I have selected represent an appropriately varied and evidence-based toolkit, and whether there are any formative assessment approaches that are particularly effective that I should add to the guide.

I have developed a complete assessment for learning cycle resource pack for department heads in a secondary school, covering the theory behind assessment for learning, practical classroom strategies, observation and coaching tools, and a school-wide implementation roadmap, and I would like your feedback on whether the resource pack is likely to support meaningful and sustainable improvement in assessment practices across the school, whether the theory is presented in a way that will be accessible and convincing to busy subject teachers, whether the practical tools are realistic to implement, and whether the implementation roadmap accounts for the organisational and cultural challenges that schools commonly face when trying to embed new assessment practices.

Could you evaluate the case study discussion activity I designed for a psychology of learning course, where students analyse a detailed description of a struggling student and must apply three different theoretical perspectives to explain the student's difficulties and propose evidence-based interventions, and tell me whether the case study is realistic and detailed enough to support rigorous theoretical analysis, whether the task instructions make the expectations clear without being overly directive, whether the activity is well-calibrated for the level of theoretical knowledge students should have at this stage of the course, and whether the debrief questions I have written are likely to prompt the kind of integrative and evaluative thinking the activity is designed to develop?

I have written a complete set of lesson materials for a design thinking workshop aimed at Year 10 students, including an introduction to the design thinking process, a real-world challenge brief, team brainstorming protocols, a prototype planning template, and a reflection activity, and I would like you to review everything and tell me whether the workshop is well-structured to take students through the design thinking process in a meaningful rather than superficial way, whether the real-world challenge is sufficiently authentic and engaging, whether the protocols and templates genuinely scaffold the thinking process, and whether the workshop could be completed within a double lesson period.

Please review the complete feedback report I wrote for a student teacher after observing three of their lessons over one week, which covers their growth since the previous observation cycle, their current strengths, specific areas for development with detailed recommendations, and agreed targets for the next observation cycle, and tell me whether the report is clearly written and evidence-based throughout, whether the balance between positive observations and developmental feedback is appropriate for a trainee who is progressing but still has significant areas to work on, whether the development targets are specific and achievable, and whether the overall tone of the report is professional, constructive, and encouraging without glossing over genuine weaknesses.

I have created a structured learning walk protocol for use by senior leaders in a large primary school, where small groups of leaders move through classrooms together to collect evidence on a specific instructional focus agreed in advance, and I would like you to review the protocol and tell me whether the observation focus is communicated clearly enough to allow consistent data collection, whether the evidence collection tool I have designed is practical to use in a busy classroom context, whether the protocol includes appropriate guidance on professional conduct during classroom visits, and whether the debrief process I have outlined is likely to generate useful insights for school improvement rather than just a list of surface-level observations.

Could you review the academic integrity workshop I designed for first-year university students, which covers the definition and purpose of academic integrity, common forms of academic misconduct, how to cite and reference correctly, how to paraphrase effectively, and the consequences of plagiarism at the institution, and tell me whether the content is appropriate and comprehensive for students entering higher education for the first time, whether the activities I have designed are likely to build genuine understanding rather than just surface compliance, whether the workshop effectively addresses the most common reasons why first-year students inadvertently commit academic misconduct, and whether the tone I have adopted is appropriately informative and non-threatening?

I have drafted a professional learning plan for a group of teachers who want to develop their skills in using data to inform differentiated instruction, spanning one full school term and including initial diagnostic activities, collaborative planning sessions, individual classroom experiments, peer observations, and a final reflection and share-out, and I would like you to assess whether the plan is likely to result in genuine and lasting changes to teaching practice, whether the time allocated to each phase is realistic given teachers' workloads, whether the plan builds appropriate professional knowledge before asking teachers to make changes in the classroom, and whether there are any elements of effective professional learning that the plan is currently missing.

Please evaluate the curriculum integration plan I wrote for introducing computational thinking skills across the Year 7 and Year 8 curriculum in subjects including mathematics, science, English, and humanities, describing specific points of integration in each subject, the skills to be addressed at each integration point, and the assessment approaches that will be used to track skill development, and tell me whether the integration points I have identified are genuinely meaningful rather than forced connections, whether the skills progression across the two year groups is coherent, whether the plan is realistic for teachers who are subject specialists without a computer science background, and whether the assessment approaches I have described are appropriate for cross-curricular skills.

I have written a guide for teachers on how to facilitate productive and inclusive whole-class discussions in secondary classrooms, covering strategies for establishing discussion norms, using questioning techniques to deepen thinking, managing turn-taking equitably, responding to incorrect or incomplete student contributions, and closing discussions in ways that consolidate learning, and I would like you to review it and tell me whether the guidance is specific and practical enough to be genuinely useful for classroom teachers, whether the strategies I recommend are supported by research evidence, whether the language and tone are appropriate for an audience of experienced secondary teachers, and whether there are any common discussion facilitation challenges that the guide fails to address.

Could you review the complete set of examination preparation materials I developed for a national high-school leaving certificate examination in biology, including a topic-by-topic content summary, a set of past paper questions organised by topic and difficulty, a set of worked model answers, examiner tip boxes highlighting common mistakes, and advice on examination technique and time management, and tell me whether the content summaries are accurate and at the right level of detail for the examination, whether the selection of past paper questions is representative of the range and style of questions students are likely to encounter, whether the model answers would genuinely help students understand what examiners are looking for, and whether the examination technique advice is specific and actionable?

I have designed a collaborative professional development experience for a cross-school teacher network focused on improving writing instruction across the curriculum, which spans three full days spread over a term and includes keynote presentations, subject-specific workshops, collaborative curriculum planning, classroom video analysis, and an action research planning session, and I would like your feedback on whether the programme has a coherent and well-justified overall design logic, whether the balance between input, discussion, and practical work is appropriate, whether the cross-school collaborative elements are structured in a way that is likely to generate productive professional exchange, and whether the programme adequately prepares teachers to apply what they have learned when they return to their schools.

Please review the detailed specification I have written for a summative portfolio assessment in a creative arts course, which asks students to compile a curated selection of their work from across the semester, write reflective commentaries for each piece, produce a personal artist statement, and present their portfolio in a public exhibition, and tell me whether the assessment tasks are well-aligned with the learning outcomes of the course, whether the instructions for each component are clear and specific enough for students to understand what is expected without being overly prescriptive about the creative choices they make, whether the assessment criteria I have provided are appropriate for evaluating creative and reflective work, and whether the public exhibition component raises any equity or inclusivity concerns that I should address.

I have written a complete facilitation guide for a student-led inquiry unit in a Year 5 class where students investigate a local environmental issue, design an awareness campaign, and present their findings and recommendations to a community panel, and I would like you to review the guide and tell me whether the inquiry framework I have used appropriately scaffolds student autonomy at this age level, whether the guidance I have provided for the community panel presentation is realistic and appropriately challenging for ten year olds, whether the teacher facilitation notes are clear and practical, whether the assessment approach I have described captures the full range of skills the unit is intended to develop, and whether there are any safeguarding or community engagement considerations I should address in the guide.

Could you evaluate the comprehensive feedback protocol I developed for a writing workshop model used in a secondary English department, which outlines how teachers should structure written feedback on student drafts, how student peers should conduct structured feedback sessions, how students should be supported to act on feedback during redrafting, and how teachers should assess the quality of student revisions, and tell me whether the protocol is coherent and well-integrated across its different components, whether the guidance for teachers and students is clear and specific, whether the protocol is realistic to implement consistently across a busy department, and whether the approach to feedback I have described is well-supported by research evidence on effective feedback in writing instruction?
